// Integrating LinkLog Contents Cheatsheet

The Python SDK · Field Guide

Integrating LinkLog

Record everything on every request. Throw it away on success at a cost too small to measure. Keep a forensic checkpoint only when something breaks.

A hands-on guide in the voice of The Clear Mentor — a patient senior engineer, example-first. For developers wiring LinkLog into a Python service with the llog SDK.

What you'll build

By the end you can drop LinkLog into a FastAPI service or a worker, capture the full execution context of a real failure, keep sensitive data on the host, and survive a failure storm — all without ever risking the app you're observing.

  1. 01 The Idea: Discard Success, Commit Failure Why LinkLog is not another logger — and the "intent vs. error" gap it keeps.
  2. 02 Getting Started: Your First Checkpoint Install, init(), one decorator, and a .llog artifact on disk.
  3. 03 Instrumenting Code: intent, scope, snapshot The three primitives that draw your causality graph.
  4. 04 Catching Failures: Middleware & the Excepthook Why middleware is primary and the global hook is only a fallback.
  5. 05 Buffer Modes: Anonymous vs Staging The one setting that decides whether you can see an OOM-kill.
  6. 06 Privacy: Allowlist Capture & the Write-Only Wall Capture only what you declare; nothing flows back into your app.
  7. 07 Surviving Failure Storms Per-signature rate limiting so 10,000 failures don't become 10,000 tickets.
  8. 08 The Guarantees: Zero-Panic, Bounded, Fast The promises that let you leave it on in production forever.
  9. 09 Downstream: The Sidecar & Replay Harness What happens to the checkpoint after your process is done with it.
  10. ★ Cheatsheet The whole API, every config knob, and the traps — on one page.
Before you start

LinkLog's core capture path uses Linux-only kernel features (O_TMPFILE, /dev/shm). It runs everywhere for local development via a fallback, but the real mechanism — and any benchmark worth trusting — lives on Linux. Chapter 2 makes this concrete.

Contents Begin ›
